data.title: TIM S.A. Q1: Good Results And Momentum Are Interesting, But Not Enough
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I am raising my recommendation from sell to hold on TIM S.A. (NYSE:TIMB) shares. This article is a continuation of my initial coverage article published on May 14 here on Seeking Alpha.

In this article, I will revisit my thesis and highlight where I was wrong. Since the publication of the article with the recommendation to sell, the total return has been 16.11%. But as Charlie Munger would say, it is important to revisit mistakes to learn from them.

Additionally, I will now analyze the Q1 results published on May 5. I will also explain the valuation and the reason for raising the recommendation from sell to hold and not buy.

TIM

TIM is one of the giants of the Brazilian telecommunications sector and has been operating in the country for 27 years. Its main competitors are Vivo (NYSE:VIV), Claro (a subsidiary of América Móvil), and recently Nu Holdings (NYSE:NU).

The company has more than 60 million mobile customers and is the leader in 4G and 5G coverage in Brazil. The company's main shareholder is Telecom Italia (OTCPK:TIIAY) with 67% of the shares.

Revisiting the Thesis

In my initial coverage article, I recommended selling due to increased competition in the sector. It is important to remember that the Brazilian population has high household debt, which in my view reduces growth.

In addition, existing players were already dealing with competition among themselves, however, the entry of Nu Holdings into this market raised concerns about intensifying competition.

It is important to remember that Nu has 100 million Brazilian customers, and its mobile phone service directly impacts TIM, which has high exposure to the mobile segment.

As we can see above, the stock has risen significantly since then. What has happened since then is that TIM's operating income has improved, and competition has not yet increased significantly.

Now, I will analyze the results that the company released on May 5th and link them with the new vision of the thesis. The company exceeded the market's net income expectations.

Net Revenue

TIM's net revenue changed +4.9% y/y and -3.6% q/q to BRL 6.39 billion ($1.12 billion). This performance was driven by the 6.2% increase in mobile services, especially in postpaid. The quarterly reduction, however, is due to seasonality.

As for perspectives, I believe that TIM will continue to reduce churn, migrate customers to more profitable plans and perform upselling. However, I remain curious about Nu Holdings' entry into this market and the impact that this will have in the future.

Given Nu's 100 million customers in Brazil, a 15% conversion of its customers to mobile phone service will represent a significant loss of market share for TIM and its competitors.

Costs, Expenses and Margins

TIM managed to control costs and expenses on an annual basis. The company's EBITDA margin changed by 0.80% y/y and -2.2% q/q. The annual increase reflects the control of operating costs, while the quarterly decrease reflects a one-off pressure from roaming and content expenses.

It is vital that the company remains efficient and closely controls costs. The industry scenario is complex, as there is little room for growth in new users, and existing users face strong competition from other players.

Debt and CapEx

The Net Debt Total/Normalized EBITDA metric reached 0.86x, we can infer that the company's debt remains healthy.

Capex fell 1.1% y/y and 2.6% q/q to BRL 1.33bn ($232m). Currently, CapEx represents 20.9% of the company's net revenue.

Investors who follow TIM's thesis should pay attention to the company's capital discipline. The business model requires significant investments in fixed assets, especially commitments to 5G. Therefore, it is extremely important to analyze in all results whether the company demonstrates efficiency in capital allocation.

Net Income

The company's normalized net income increased by +56% y/y and -23.2% q/q to BRL 810 million ($142 million). The annual expansion was driven by expense control and improved operating income.

I assess the 1Q25 results as interesting, given that the company managed to improve revenues, costs, and expenses, and its results were driven entirely by efficiency. Before we change our recommendation, let's talk about valuation.

Valuation

To value the company, we will use the P/E multiple. In this sector, I see many investors using EV/EBITDA as their valuation, but this is wrong, as these companies have inflated depreciation and amortization due to the nature of their activity. This gives a false impression of high EBITDA and leaves the multiple artificially low.

As we can see, the tool indicates a favorable valuation. However, I see that the best way to evaluate the company is through the P/E FWD against the sector itself. We have the sector trading at 12.67x earnings versus the company trading at 11.51x earnings, and then we have an upside of 10%.

These results and the 10% upside are enough for me to upgrade the recommendation from sell to hold. However, I still see many challenges regarding the competition, little growth potential, and an upside not large enough to justify a buy recommendation.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

I see two major risks to the thesis, remembering that the risk is that the investor will not buy the stock and miss out on new highs. The first risk is related to the company's profile.

Brazil is currently experiencing a complex macroeconomic environment and extremely high interest rates. TIM operates in a defensive sector and may attract investor buying flow in a more hostile economic environment.

As we have seen, the company has high operating margins and healthy debt. If TIM maintains disciplined capital allocation, it could increase the dividends distributed, which could also attract investors seeking dividends.

Last but not least, Seeking Alpha’s powerful Quant tools recommend a strong buy on the stock. This goes against my skeptical recommendation to hold.

The Bottom Line

TIM reported good results in Q1. Despite the competition, the company increased net revenue by 4.9% y/y and had an 80 bps y/y increase in EBITDA margin, showing rigidity in cost and expense control.

However, I have seen investors using the wrong method to evaluate the stock. The EV/EBITDA indicator can give a wrong view, and the best option is to analyze it using P/E, which provides a relatively small upside of 10%.

Based on this analysis, I am raising my recommendation to sell to hold the stock. In my view, the effects of the competitive environment have not yet been reflected in the results, and the upside is too small to take the risk.

data.title: Hafnia Limited: Why It's Still A Great Option
data.description:
On January 12th of this year, I wrote a report on Hafnia Limited (NYSE:HAFN) in which I provided an initial overview of the company and the strengths I identified relative to the current state of the industry. In that report, titled "Hafnia Limited: Not Just a Dividend Paying Machine", I explored the current structure of fossil fuel transportation globally and how the company is positioned in this particular scenario.

In this report, I intend to provide an update on the investment thesis developed during early 2025, as well as a brief summary of the company's new results published for 4Q2024. In this regard, I intend to focus on the development of the conflict in the Red Sea and the Suez Canal, the new regulations from the Office of Foreign Asset Control (OFAC), and how these could potentially affect Hafnia and, ultimately, the current state of the market in general.

In my opinion, and despite the ups and downs on the international scene following Trump's arrival at the White House, Hafnia remains a highly attractive option. The company continues to deliver high shareholder returns through dividend payments and share buybacks. At the same time, it demonstrates solid management with a capital structure in the process of deleveraging.

Update

I would like to begin updating the investment thesis with one of the points I highlighted in the previous report, which was the relationship between transported tons and international freight prices for different types of ships. As we mentioned at the time, it was clear that over time, there was a correlation between the number of tons transported and freight rates per vessel.

In January 2025, there was still a spread between the two, something we said would be corrected. Looking at Hafnia's latest report, we see that for LR1 vessels, this gap narrowed significantly. It can still be seen in other segments, but we estimate that this will also tend to normalize over the course of the year. During 4Q2024, the cause of the drop in transported tonnage can be traced to the closure of the Suez Canal in the Red Sea during this period. The drop in transported cargo led to a drop in freight prices, but as the situation normalized, so did prices.

Red Sea Scenario

This brings us to the topic of the closure of the Suez Canal as a result of the Red Sea crisis. In this regard, the previous report mentioned something about it, but I think it's important to delve deeper into the conflict, given the consequences it had for the company and the circumstantial changes in the international landscape.

Focusing solely on the fossil fuel transport market, it can be seen that with the closure of the Suez Canal, trade flow began a downward trend when evaluating the number of barrels transported per day. The question of why this maritime passage is important is entirely valid at this point, and I would like to briefly review it to contextualize the situation. The Suez Canal emerged as an alternative to crossing the waterways between Europe and the Middle East, so its importance for the fossil fuel industry can be seen. Even so, the canal is an alternative to shipping around the Cape of Good Hope for two fundamental reasons. First, because it allows the passage of larger vessels and because its infrastructure supports a much higher shipping flow. Second, because it shortens travel times, allowing for lower costs.

The closure of the Suez Canal due to a series of conflicts with a group of Houthi pirates in the Red Sea forced shipping companies to re-arrange cargo and resume routes that cross the Cape of Good Hope to Southern Africa. This generated a sharp increase in shipping prices, which was not fully translated into increased profitability, given the proportional increase in costs. On the other hand, in the case of the oil and gas industry, it led to a significant decrease in the number of barrels transported per day.

Now, it is important to determine what impact a resolution of the conflict and the reopening of transport through the Suez Canal could have on Hafnia. Initially, as can be seen, profits would be lower, but an increase in transported volume is also expected. All of this would generate a slightly negative, but practically neutral, impact. Evaluating this scenario becomes important following Trump's arrival at the White House. The American president seeks to end international conflicts, and the problems in the Red Sea are no exception. In fact, the Houthis are already intending to end the conflict, according to reports from Washington.

At this point, the loss the company would appear to incur in the short term if the conflict is resolved doesn't seem significant. This is especially true because in the medium term, once international trade is regularized, it's likely that even though charter rates will decrease, so will transportation costs. At the same time, I expect an increase in volume and less wear and tear on vessels, which in the long run will mean financial savings for companies like Hafnia.

OFAC Listing of Vessels & Sanctions

Another key point to consider was the sanctions imposed by the Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) on a series of vessels linked to trade with Iran. The decision was made due to suspicions that the vessels were transporting Iranian oil, a country sanctioned by the United States and banned from trade with a large number of nations. The practices allegedly involved the transfer of oil from one vessel to another in areas near Iraq, Iran, the UAE, and the Gulf of Oman.

According to the company, this OFAC vessel listing imposes a mismatch between global supply and demand, as it entails sanctioning a fleet equivalent to the entire list of new vessels scheduled for 2025. It is important to note that the sanctioned vessels are an average of 17 years old, which could imply a de facto conversion of these units to scrap. While this has clear impacts on older vessels, it also affects vessels with new technology, since, rather than complementing older vessels, they must fulfill the role of replacing technology more quickly than the market expected.

On the other hand, the company estimates that there are a potential 410 vessels around the world that will have traded with Russia by 2024 and are therefore susceptible to being added to the sanctions list. This scenario would add even more pressure to this market. On the other hand, both China and India are expected to discontinue trade relations with vessels involved in hydrocarbon imports. Hafnia estimates that this decision would involve replacing the equivalent of 100 Suezmax vessels to meet the requirements of both countries.

This reinforces one of the theories we mentioned in the previous report: the fact that vessels are becoming obsolete faster than the industry can manufacture new ones. This reality could lead to a decrease in the number of tons available for transport, which would raise prices if we take into account that international charter prices can be viewed as equivalent to several commodities.

Demand & Supply Update

The last point I'd like to highlight is an update on the current global supply and demand context in relation to the fossil fuel transportation market. What can be seen is that in the first half of the year, refineries are maintaining higher production than in the same period in 2022, 2024, and 2025. On the other hand, what can also be seen is that vessel utilization is at average levels of recent years. This, as Hafnia also points out, could boost demand.

In this regard, Søren Winther, the company's Vice President of Sales, stated the following in the last conference call:

Moving on to Slide 14. Overall, we maintain a constructive outlook for the product tanker segment into 2025. We continue to observe an increasing average age of the global product tanker fleet, and this trend has significant implications as older vessels are underutilized due to customer preference for younger tonnage. Global oil demand also remains robust. For the full year 2024, global oil demand has increased by 0.87 million barrels per day with a further increase of 1.1 million barrels per day to be expected for 2025.

Additionally, refinery maintenance accumulated at the start of the year are expected to be lower in the next months, paving the way for higher refinery runs. This will likely result in increased production and export of refined products, boosting global demand for product tankers.

4Q2024 Results

With the investment thesis update complete, I'd like to continue with the 4Q2024 results. While it's true that margins contracted sharply last quarter, both the annualized ROE and ROIC show values ​​that, in my view, are very solid. In the former case, the annualized ROE reached 34.5% while the ROIC was 25%.

If we analyze the metrics for 4Q2024, we see that the company's net profit was $79.6 million, compared to $176.4 million in 4Q2023, which implies a decline that cannot be ignored. However, I would like to focus on the company's annual metrics and compare them to 2023. In 2024, Hafnia achieved a Net Profit of $774 million, compared to $793.3 million in 2023. This equates to a profit per share of $1.52.

Looking at Adjusted EBITDA, we see a similar trend. Fourth quarter 2024 closed at $131.2 million, compared to $234.5 million in fourth quarter 2023. However, when considering the full year, Adjusted EBITDA for 2024 was $992.3 million, compared to $1,012.9 million in 2023. At current values, the EV/EBITDA ratio shows an impressive value of 3.6x, something that, in my opinion, is extremely attractive if we add up all the potential described above.

One of the positive aspects I had highlighted that reversed slightly this quarter was the evolution of net debt, on the one hand, and the value of Hafnia's fleet, on the other. One of the things I had previously highlighted was the company's deleveraging due to an increase in cash, which had led to a significant reduction in net debt. This was even more surprising considering that this had occurred in a context of increasing fleet value. Both processes were slightly eroded in this last quarter. I think this is not a cause for concern at the moment, but it is worth monitoring how it evolves in the future.

Risks

Before concluding this report, I would like to briefly mention the potential risks the company may face in the future. First, and in line with the latter, the decline in the fleet's value could be a factor to consider. If OFAC continues to take action and the sanctioned vessels are treated as scrap due to their age, this would jeopardize the value of similar vessels held by companies like Hafnia.

Secondly, if international oil prices continue to decline, we could enter a reversal of the trend. Given this change in international crude oil prices, refineries could have less incentive to produce, leading to a reduction in demand for vessels transporting processed fuels.

Finally, although there has been news in recent days that appears to suggest the United States will halt tariff increases on imports, the issue is still ongoing. Should these barriers to international trade be reactivated, it will undoubtedly have a negative impact on global markets, and the shipping industry will be no exception.

Conclusion

Despite the caveats I mentioned previously, I believe the outlook described in the first part, which delves into the thesis detailed in the previous report, is more than interesting. I view Hafnia positively for the medium term, and I believe the combination of good management, an appropriate margin of safety, a solvent financial position, a favorable international environment, and the strong dividend payouts I mentioned in the previous report continues to make Hafnia an excellent option to hold in your portfolio. For this reason, I believe the company continues to represent a strong buy.

data.title: 3 Great REITs For Buy-And-Hold Investors
data.description:
REITs have done poorly in recent years, and this is causing investors to forget just how exceptionally rewarding they have been over the long run.

Taking the longest possible time frame, which is over 50 years, REITs have easily outperformed most other asset classes, including the S&P 500 (SPY), Tech stocks (QQQ), bonds, and gold (GLD):

Moreover, these averages include the good, the bad, and the ugly.

Some specific REIT business models were a lot more rewarding than the average, earning closer to 15-20% average annual returns.

Today, even those highly lucrative REITs are priced at historically low valuations, and in what follows, I will highlight three such REITs to buy and hold "forever".

Their business models are like magic. They create far more shareholder value than others, resulting in persistent outperformance.

CubeSmart (CUBE)

Over the long run, the most rewarding property sector has been self-storage.

Those REITs have earned near-20% average annual returns for the 28-year period ending in 2021:

They have been so incredibly rewarding because of three key reasons:

1) The large scale of REITs was a major advantage in this particular property sector as it allowed them to develop valuable brands (think "Public Storage"), better marketing strategies with national advertising campaigns, and superior pricing models.

2) They were able to develop a lot of new properties at high initial yields as demand was growing, resulting in large spreads over their cost of capital.

3) They were also able to acquire properties from smaller, less sophisticated private landlords, and then change brands and improve operations to rapidly create a lot of value.

And CubeSmart has done even better than the average of its peer group.

It has managed to consistently grow faster and earn better returns:

We think that CubeSmart stands out for three key reasons, which we expect to lead to more outperformance over the long run:

1) Its properties are located in the most dense markets, where people live in small homes and real estate is expensive. Brooklyn would be a good example. As a result, these properties enjoy strong and growing demand, but their supply is strictly limited:

2) CubeSmart also operates a capital-light management business, providing its scale, brand, and expertise to other smaller self-storage property owners. It's win-win because CubeSmart is often able to boost profits for the landlord, increase their property value, turn it into a passive business, and they then earn fees for it.

3) Finally, CubeSmart is the right size. It is big enough to enjoy economies of scale, but not too big to slow down its growth. Its market cap is $9 billion, but it is still much smaller than its close peers like Public Storage (PSA) and Extra Space Storage (EXR). The smaller size should allow it to grow faster over the long run.

Despite all of that, CubeSmart has tanked with the rest of the REIT market in this 3-year-long bear market, and now trades at a historically low valuation:

Its price to FFO is just 16x, which is a discount relative to its lower quality peers:

FFO Multiple
CubeSmart 16x
Public Storage 18x
Extra Space Storage 18x

We think that this is an opportunity for long-term oriented investors to build a position in a high-quality REIT that should outperform the broader market with less risk over the long run.

The ~5% dividend yield, coupled with its 6-9% average annual long-term growth, should be enough to deliver double-digit total returns, and the REIT could enjoy another 20% upside as it reprices at closer to 20x FFO, which has historically been the norm for this REIT.

Lastly, the REIT has a strong BBB investment grade rating and is not heavily impacted by the surge in interest rates.

Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR)

The second-best performing property sector over the long run has been industrial.

It has earned a near 16% average annual total return:

The property sector did so well because it benefited from the rapid growth of e-commerce.

This trend continues today and could even accelerate with the AI revolution, as it will allow e-commerce companies to improve their marketing, cut down on costs, offer even lower prices, and faster and cheaper shipping.

The AI revolution is also making it way easier to start new e-commerce businesses, which is expected to lead to a boom in new business formation, all of which will need some industrial space.

At the same time, industrial landlords also benefit from the growing trend of onshoring, which will only accelerate with AI and robotics, as it will close the gap in manufacturing costs. This is even more true with the tariffs being imposed.

So the long-term demand for industrial space looks bright. Yet, these REITs are today discounted because the sector is temporarily oversupplied:

The good news is that the new construction activity has dropped to a historically low level as a result of the high interest rates and construction costs.

This should lead to a strong acceleration in rent growth in the coming years and remove the overhang that has hurt the market sentiment of these REITs.

And Rexford Industrial stands out in this property sector.

It has done even better than the average of industrial REITs over the long run:

Its outperformance is the result of its focus on Southern California, which is commonly perceived to be the strongest industrial market in the world.

It is strategically located right by the Pacific and the Mexican border. Therefore, it is the entry and exit to the United States for importers and exporters. California is also a major consumer economy.

It is resulting in growing demand for space, but the new supply is highly constrained because most land is already built out:

Therefore, I think that REXR could be one of the biggest winners in the AI revolution. It owns highly desirable properties that will grow in demand and enjoy significant barriers to entry.

Yet, the REIT is today heavily discounted, trading at its lowest valuation in a long time:

This chart may lead you to think that the company is facing severe headwinds, but in reality, its cash flows and dividends are hitting new all-time highs. The company just released its Q1 results, and it grew its FFO per share by another 6.9%.

Yes, its growth has slowed down due to the oversupply, but since this is just temporary, it is resulting in a great opportunity for long-term oriented investors.

The stock now trades at just 15x FFO, down from over 40x at its peak.

You earn a 5% dividend yield that's well-covered, and the REIT is expected to grow at around 7-8% per year over the long run, resulting in double-digit total returns.

Historically, the REIT has also commonly traded at 20-25x FFO, meaning that it has at least 25% upside to its fair value.

VICI Properties (VICI)

Talking about properties that enjoy significant barriers-to-entry, it is hard to beat VICI Properties.

It is the leading casino net lease REIT, and it owns properties like the Caesars Palace, the Venetian, and the MGM Grand on the Las Vegas strip:

These are truly one-of-a-kind properties. The Las Vegas strip is already built out, and these properties require licenses that are very difficult to obtain.

Over the long run, this unique focus on high-quality casinos has resulted in significant market outperformance:

But today, the stock is relatively cheap, likely because the market seems to fear that we could be headed into a recession.

However, what it is overlooking is that VICI is a triple net lease REIT, meaning that its properties are under very long leases that result in highly consistent and predictable cash flow.

Its average remaining lease term is over 40 years. The leases include annual rent escalations and inflation protection. Tenants are even responsible for all property expenses, including maintenance.

As a result, its cash flow was resilient even to the pandemic, which was the worst possible crisis for a casino operator.

Its tenants are highly dependent on these properties, and they keep paying their rent in full and on time because they have no other option but to keep these assets under their control.

VICI is offered today at 12x FFO. It pays a 5.5% dividend yield, and it should be able to grow by about 5% per year over the long run.

The yield plus the growth should again result in double-digit total returns, and there is potential for a bit of upside from multiple expansion on top of that. This should be enough for VICI to keep outperforming the market, and that is why it is a core holding in our portfolio at High Yield Landlord.

data.title: TopBuild: Insulating Profits With A Valuation You Can Warm Up To
data.description:
Introduction

TopBuild Corp. (NYSE:BLD) is a leading installer and specialty distributor of insulation and building material products, serving the construction industry across residential, commercial, and industrial markets. With a nationwide footprint, the company operates through two primary segments, Installation and Specialty Distribution, generating approximately $5.2 billion in annual revenue.

I was first attracted to TopBuild as I was looking for ideas in the homebuilding and building materials space. Acquisitions like the one AZEK Company (AZEK) did for James Hardie (JHX) for $8.8 billion (which I previously covered here) and Beacon Roofing Supply getting acquired for $11 billion by QXO Inc. (QXO) are just a few of the many acquisitions that have been announced so far this year.

While it's anyone's guess as to which company could be next, I think TopBuild could fit the bill given its strong market position, diversified business model across installation and specialty distribution, an M&A pipeline rolling up smaller companies, and a wide discount to other peers in the space. In this article, I’ll break down TopBuild’s latest Q1’25 results that were reported on May 6 and share my take on TopBuild’s outlook and growth potential in its key markets and why I see the stock as a ‘buy’ based on its compelling valuation.

Q1’25 Overview

TopBuild reported a great quarter for Q1’25 with results that exceeded estimates on both the top and bottom line. While revenue declined 3.6% year over year to $1.2 billion, this still represented a beat of $3.3 million (right in line with consensus) and was understandable given the challenging macro environment, especially the residential construction sector. The sales drop was a function of a 7.4% drop-off in volumes, but that was partially offset by a 2.6% contribution from acquisitions and a 1.2% pricing increase.

Moving down the income statement, gross margins contracted by about 70bps to 29.6%, mostly due to pricing pressure on residential distribution products like spray foam. On SG&A, the company’s total SG&A as a percent of revenues climbed by 40bps to 13.9%. Ultimately, that led to a decrease in net income per share with EPS of $4.23 (or $4.63 adjusted), but this was still 23 cents ahead of Wall Street’s expectations.

When we look at the company’s two primary segments, the Installation segment that accounts for almost two-thirds of revenue, saw a 6.7% sales decline to $745.5 million, with volume down 9.6% as a result of pressures in single-family, multifamily, and light commercial markets, though the heavy commercial market provided some offset. In contrast, the Specialty Distribution segment (which represents the remaining 38% of revenue) grew sales by 2.6% to $559.8 million, thanks to good performance in the commercial and industrial, like mechanical insulation, despite some softer residential demand.

Key takeaways and highlights from the quarter

On the whole, I think the company’s quarter was decent all things considered on the macro. From a profitability perspective, the company showed a bit of margin resilience as adjusted EBITDA came in at $234.8 million with a margin of 19.0%, down 80bps from last year. By segment, the Installation segment’s margin fell 90bps to 21.1% and Specialty Distribution’s margin declined 60bps, to 16.3%. Given that SG&A in dollar terms was relatively stable (in dollar terms) year over year, I think the company had better operating leverage than what investors were expecting. As well, because the company bought back stock, EPS wasn’t hurt too badly, given the number of diluted shares went down by 2.6 million shares.

In terms of some of the key highlights from the quarter, the company has been working on a number of footprint optimization projects as they consolidate 33 facilities across both of their segments. For that, they had $13.9 million in one-time costs (most of which were a non-cash lease payment). While it’s been a long process and has some one-time initial cost, the consolidation, as well as headcount reductions, are expected to generate annual savings of $30 million or more, according to management.

With respect to the balance sheet and capital allocation, TopBuild is in a good spot, with total liquidity standing at $746.4 million. Net debt was $1.07 billion, which, against EBITDA, represents about 1.0x turns leverage. Given that the company’s end markets have a bit of cyclicality and the company’s business isn’t exactly recurring revenue in nature, it’s good to have some financial flexibility with low leverage. In the case of TopBuild, I think that’s especially important given the company’s capital allocation opportunities as they relate to M&A. For example, the $15 million of Seal-Rite that was closed in April was done without issuing new debt, and ample free cash flow meant that $215.6 million could be returned via buybacks. With $972.4 million remaining under its share repurchase authorization, I’m not sure if the company will reach the full amount by the end of the year, but at the very least should be able to make a meaningful dent in buying back stock at low prices, increasing the economic interest of existing shareholders. On the earnings call following the quarter, management noted a healthy pipeline of opportunities across residential and C&I segments, and so I’d expect a few more deals to be announced by year-end.

TopBuild is well-positioned

In terms of my expectations for TopBuild going forward, it’s helpful to look at the guidance given by management for FY’25. On the company’s conference call following the results, management maintained its full-year sales guidance of between $5.05 billion to $5.35 billion, implying a year-on-year change of down 5% to flat, with adjusted EBITDA guidance of $925 million to $1.075 billion, translating to margins of 18.3% to 20.1%, which will almost certainly be lower than the 20.2% achieved in FY’24. Given where the company is guiding with expectations for a high-single-digit decline in the residential business, I think the guidance is probably in line with reality and reflects management’s caution on the macro outlook. This makes sense given the weaker spring selling season and persistent softness in single-family (now expected down low single digits) and multifamily (down around 30%). In contrast, the commercial and industrial segment is expected to grow in low single digits, leaning toward the higher end, buoyed by projects moving into production and robust bid activity in sectors like data centers and healthcare.

Based on the limited visibility of April and a bit of May, TopBuild anticipates Q2’25 to exhibit the largest sales decline on a year-over-year basis, likely mirroring the first quarter’s 4% drop because of strong comps and ongoing residential weakness. In Q3, I’d expect somewhat of a recovery with a less severe decline, while Q4 should be flat to slightly down (per commentary on the earnings call), benefiting from easier comps as demand softened late last year. As EBITDA margins generally follow typical seasonality, Q2 and Q3 should be stronger contributors to FY’25 numbers, and so what we see next quarter will dictate whether guidance was too soft or not. In the short term, there’s still some uncertainty, but I feel a lot of that is largely misplaced. For example, on the earnings call, CEO Robert Buck noted that in the C&I segment, “folks have just come to accept the current financing environment and some of the bigger projects… were justified… we’ve seen many of those come online”. This highlights why delayed projects are now progressing, reinforcing my optimism about the segment’s near-term contribution.

My reason for recommending a ‘buy’ rating isn’t just based on what I think will or won’t happen next quarter or the one after. Over the longer term, I feel TopBuild’s diversified business model and strategic focus on M&A position it well for growth. Even in a volatile housing market, the company has a strong balance sheet with which it can take advantage of a healthy M&A pipeline, and the $15 million Seal-Rite deal is likely going to be just the start of that. On the earnings call, management emphasized expanding their TAM and securing strong supply chain relationships, which I think bode well for top and bottom-line growth, respectively, going forward.

Risks to consider

One of the risks I see with TopBuild as it relates to achieving its 2025 guidance would be prolonged weakness in the residential construction market, which accounts for roughly 65% of TopBuild’s revenue. Management’s revised outlook now anticipates a high single-digit decline in residential sales, driven by a weak spring selling season, elevated interest rates, and eroded consumer confidence. This softness could deepen if economic uncertainty persists or if homebuyer affordability worsens, and that could lead to pressure volumes, and therefore margins. In my view, the risk is amplified by TopBuild’s exposure to regional markets like Florida and Texas, which management noted as slower, and a failure to see stabilization in these areas could result in sales falling below the low end of guidance.

The other risk I’d say would be execution challenges in the commercial and industrial segment. While the segment has shown resilience so far as projects move into production and strong bid activity is being seen in data centers and healthcare, the potential impact of tariffs could re-escalate and disrupt TopBuild’s cost structure and pricing strategy. Management estimates the impact of announced tariffs at less than 5% of the cost of goods sold, so exposure is limited for now.

TopBuild's valuation is inexpensive

Analysts are reasonably bullish on TopBuild’s outlook over the next twelve months. With 12 analysts covering the stock, there are 8 ‘buy’ ratings and 4 ‘hold’ ratings. Collectively, the analysts have an average price target of $349.73 which implies about 16.6% upside from current levels.

Overall, I’d say that the stock represents good value at its current price around $300 a share. At today’s prices, investors are paying 12.6 P/E and 9.9 EV/EBITDA. Compared to its historical average multiple of 14.6x EV/EBITDA, this seems like a good entry point.

As well, we can see from the chart below that TopBuild trades below the peer group multiples of 12.6x EV/EBITDA and 20.3x P/E. At a 3 turn discount on EV/EBITDA and a 5 turn discount on P/E, I find it hard to understand why the market is assigning a lower valuation multiple to what I’d consider to be an above-average quality business for a company in its industry. Compared to its closest peer, Installed Building Products (IBP), I think this could be due to the fact that IBP has more consistent organic growth and a strong presence in the residential new construction market. Because they focus more on installation services (e.g., insulation), they have more margin stability.

In my view, I think that the discount between the two has become too wide at this point. While TopBuild should trade at some discount, the case for buying the company today, in my view, rests on the fact that you can buy a business with both distribution with supply chain control and pricing power that can enhance margins over time. In other words, the margin upside is greater with a company like TopBuild. Finally, TopBuild has a strong track record of value-accretive acquisitions, growing its EPS from $2.10 a decade ago to $19.89 today.

Takeaway

In summary, I think TopBuild stands out as a well-managed, strategically positioned player in the building materials space with a balanced business model spanning both installation and specialty distribution. Despite short-term headwinds in the residential construction market, I think the company's latest results showed decent cost management and an ability to remain profitable even in a tougher environment. I view the company's strong balance sheet, active M&A pipeline, and disciplined capital allocation strategy as support for a compelling long-term investment thesis. Trading at a discount to peers despite strong fundamentals and upside margin potential, TopBuild offers investors an attractive entry point, which is why I think it's a ‘buy’ at current levels. Long BLD.

data.title: Baron Opportunity Fund Q1 2025 Top Contributors And Detractors
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Baron Opportunity Fund Q1 2025 Shareholder Letter.

Top Contributors to Performance

Table II.

Top contributors to performance for the quarter ended March 31, 2025

Contribution to Return (%)
X.AI Holdings Corp. 0.70
Spotify Technology S.A. (SPOT) 0.55
Inari Medical, Inc. 0.40
CoStar Group, Inc. (CSGP) 0.20
Visa Inc. (V) 0.17

Founded by Elon Musk in March 2023 with the ambitious mission “to understand the true nature of the universe,” X.AI Holdings Corp. (xAI), has quickly emerged as a prominent force in the AI industry. In a remarkably short time span, xAI released to the market its AI model, Grok, and recently followed up with the high-profile release of Grok 3, marking a major advancement in its technology. Fueling Grok 3’s breakthrough performance was the rapid deployment of xAI’s Colossus data center. Redefining what’s possible, Colossus became operational in just 122 days, running a highly utilized fleet of 100,000 GPUs. It currently holds the title of one of – if not the – largest coherent AI training infrastructure. The next major milestone, scaling to 200,000 GPUs, was achieved in a mere 92 days. Grok 3 was the first model to be trained on xAI’s own infrastructure, utilizing over 10 times the computational power used for Grok 2. These early milestones demonstrate xAI’s capacity to accelerate innovation and its potential to secure a sustained leadership role in the highly competitive AI industry. Further to these remarkable achievements, xAI recently announced that it has acquired X, formerly Twitter. The acquisition is expected to result in meaningful efficiencies and growth opportunities, driven by better alignment of corporate incentives, enhanced resource allocation, and the integration of data, compute, and products. While some competitors struggle to secure high-quality data, the acquisition is expected to enhance xAI’s access to X’s vast, real-time, multimodal data, generated by 600 million active users worldwide. We anticipate that xAI’s AI products will be more seamlessly integrated into the X platform, enhancing the user experience and creating improved distribution opportunities for the company’s offerings. We value the stock based on transaction in shares, including the recently announced merger, resulting in stock appreciation and quarterly outperformance.

Spotify Technology S.A. is the leading global digital music platform, offering on-demand audio streaming through both paid premium subscriptions and an ad-supported tier. Shares of Spotify rose following another strong quarterly result, marked by a significant gross margin beat and healthy expansion in operating margins. The company’s compelling value proposition has allowed it to begin flexing pricing power, introducing additional price increases across various global markets. Notably, user growth remains robust despite these hikes, underscoring the strength of the platform. We believe Spotify will continue to leverage its pricing power as it enhances its offering with new features such as audiobooks, AI-driven personalization, a forthcoming “Super Premium” tier, and potentially an education-focused product. In addition to a solid top line and improving margins, the company generated a record $2.3 billion in free cash flow in 2024, reflecting growing scale and discipline in execution. Looking ahead, we see a clear path toward 35% gross margins, driven by continued growth in high-margin areas such as marketplace, advertising, podcasts, and audiobooks. Operating efficiency remains a strategic focus. Compared to many other consumer discretionary and tech names, Spotify has traded more defensively, aided by the platform’s strong consumer retention and resilient financial characteristics. We expect the company to sustain positive earnings growth, supported by both company-specific drivers and broader industry tailwinds. The launch of the Super Premium tier – expected this year in North America and globally – should be materially accretive to profitability and free cash flow over time. We continue to view Spotify as a long-term winner in music and audio streaming, with the potential to scale to over 1 billion monthly active users.

Inari Medical, Inc. supplies catheter-based devices to remove clots from venous thromboembolism (VTE). VTE is the third most common vascular condition in the U.S. after heart attacks and strokes and can be fatal if left untreated. The market is still mostly greenfield, with patients receiving only ineffective drugs, and we believe there is room for multiple devices to win. Shares contributed to performance after Inari was acquired by Stryker (SYK) in early January.

Top Detractors from Performance

Table III.

Top detractors from performance for the quarter ended March 31, 2025

Contribution to Return (%)
Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) -1.98
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) -1.81
Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) -1.35
Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) -0.90
Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) -0.88

Nvidia Corporation is a semiconductor and systems company specializing in compute and networking systems for accelerated computing. Its unmatched leadership in AI infrastructure, spanning GPUs, systems, software and networking solutions, continues to drive robust performance. However, Nvidia’s stock came under pressure during the quarter, as media and investor narratives shifted toward skepticism, ranging from concerns over slower AI adoption to DeepSeek-related fears that future AI training and inference workloads may become more compute-efficient, reducing demand of accelerated computing systems. As discussed above, we believe these concerns are premature. Training cluster buildouts are progressing in line with expectations, while inference will progressively and steadily scale with usage as enterprises integrate AI into real-world workflows and consumers continue to adopt AI applications, such as ChatGPT, Grok, and Perplexity, to name just a few. Moreover, as we shift from standard gen 1 (gut based) AI models to reasoning gen 2 (long thinking) models, the query response can demand about 100 times more inference compute to provide a better answer. In contrast to these skeptical narratives, Nvidia delivered a strong January 2025 quarter, which exceeded Street expectations, driven by data center compute revenues growing 116% to $32.6 billion, with $11 billion of revenue from Nvidia’s new Blackwell architecture, the fastest product ramp in the company’s history. On the February earnings call and at the GTC conference in March, CEO Jensen Huang reiterated a number of NVIDA growth drivers, including: (1) accelerated (GPU-based) computing architectures replacing legacy (CPU-based) computing architectures; (2) multiple Gen AI scaling laws, including pre-training (more data, more compute, smarter models), post-training using reinforcement learning from human and AI feedback, and inference with test-time, long-reasoning compute; (3) agentic AI (autonomous, non-human workers); and (4) physical AI (robots, EV’s, etc.).

Tesla, Inc. designs, manufactures, and sells fully EVs, related software and components, and solar and energy storage products. Shares fell due to declining analyst expectations for auto delivery volumes and gross margins in 2025 due to: (1) a refresh of the Model Y, Tesla’s highest-volume vehicle and the world’s best-selling car in 2024; (2) Elon Musk’s controversial role in the Trump administration as putative leader of DOGE; and (3) regulatory changes that could pose potential operational challenges. Despite these headwinds, we remain confident in Tesla’s long-term growth, underpinned by secular trends in EVs and energy storage adoption, a compelling and expanding product line, its leading cost structure, and cutting-edge, real-world AI technologies, particularly autonomous driving and humanoid robots. We believe the Model Y refresh alongside the debut of new mass-market models should boost vehicle demand as we move through the year. Over time, we expect the political pressure to fade, while Tesla’s AI ambitions – a robotaxi service launching this year and the Optimus humanoid program – hold the promise of transforming its growth story. Here’s are some direct quotes from Elon on Tesla’s late-January earnings call:

  • Full Self Driving: “[V]ery few people understand the value of full self-driving and our ability to monetize the fleet…[T]hat’s why my number one recommendation for anyone who doubts is simply try it…[T]he only people who are skeptical…are those who have not tried it. So a … passenger car typically has only about 10 hours of utility per week out of 168, a very small percentage. Once that car is autonomous, my rough estimate is that it is in use for at least a third of the hours per week, so call it, 50, maybe 55 hours of the week. And it can be useful both cargo delivery and people delivery. So even let’s say people are asleep, but you can deliver packages in the middle of the night, or resupply restaurants, or whatever the case may be, whatever people need at all hours of the day or night. That same asset … that already exist[a] with no incremental cost change, just a software update, now ha[s] 5x or more the utility than they currently have. I think this will be the largest asset value increase in human history.”
  • Optimus: “With regard to Optimus, obviously, I’m making these revenue predictions that sound absolutely insane. I realize that. But I think they will prove to be accurate. Now, with Optimus, there’s a lot of uncertainty on the exact timing because it’s not like a train arriving at the station for Optimus. We are designing the train and the station and in real time while also building the tracks…The normal internal plan calls for roughly 10,000 Optimus robots to be built this year. Will we succeed in building 10,000 exactly by the end of December this year? Probably not. But will we succeed in making several thousand?... Will those several thousand Optimus robots be doing useful things by the end of the year? Yes, I’m confident they will do useful things. Those Optimus in use at the Tesla factories for production design one will inform how would we change for production design two, which we expect to launch next year. And our goal is to ramp up Optimus production faster than maybe anything has ever been ramped… We tried desperately with Optimus to use any existing motors, any actuators, sensors, nothing worked for a humanoid robot at any price. We had to design everything from physics first principles to work for a humanoid robot. And with the most sophisticated hand that has ever been made before, by far. Optimus will be able to, like, play the piano and be able to thread a needle. I mean, this is the level of precision no one has been able to achieve. And so, it’s really something special…So… my prediction long-term is that Optimus will be overwhelmingly the value of the company… I’m confident at 1 million units a year that the production cost of Optimus will be less than $20,000. If you compare the complexity of Optimus to the complexity of a car, so just the total mass and complexity of Optimus is much less than a car. So I would expect that at similar volumes to, say, the Model Y, which is over 1 million units a year, that you’d see Optimus be…half the cost or something like that. What the price of Optimus is, is a different matter. The price of Optimus will be set by the market demand.”

Broadcom Inc. is a leading fabless semiconductor and enterprise software company, with approximately 60% of revenue generated from semiconductors and 40% from software. The company’s focus on high-performance AI compute and networking solutions, coupled with its disciplined execution in software, positions it as a strategic leader in critical technology markets. Broadcom delivered a strong quarter, beating expectations on both semiconductors and software. AI remained the key growth engine – reaching a $4.1 billion quarterly run rate, up 77% year-over-year – driving a solid ramp in semiconductor revenue, even as non-AI segments like wireless and industrial declined. Software surged, fueled by VMware integration and a shift to subscriptions. Management guided to continued AI momentum and steady software execution, with a more pronounced AI ramp expected in the second half. Despite strong quarterly results, the stock retreated amid the same AI-related skepticism as Nvidia, but primarily due to the broad market pullback on tariff and trade-relations concerns. We remain confident Broadcom is on track to win with its three custom AI accelerator customers and will capture the majority share in the custom-compute space. Broadcom’s lead customer, Google, just rolled out its 7th generation TPU, nicknamed Ironwood, and as Broadcom’s two unnamed (but well speculated) non-Google customers gain traction, confidence in the broader pipeline of four additional partners should strengthen, supporting continued growth in Broadcom’s AI business. We spent two hours with Broadcom’s CEO Hock Tan in March, and he told us in no uncertain terms that “all of them will aggressively ramp and push custom accelerators.”

data.title: VAW: Materials Dashboard For May
data.description:
This monthly reports tracks metrics in the materials sector, aiming at a top-down analysis based on value, quality, and momentum. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as Materials Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLB) and Vanguard Materials Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VAW), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for packaging in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in packaging companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios ((EY, SY, FY)) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios ((ROE, GM)) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

data.title: Workhorse Group Inc. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Workhorse Group Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: LFGY: A Strong Alternative To Crypto Stocks
data.description:
Introduction

The YieldMax Crypto Industry & Tech Portfolio Income ETF (NYSEARCA:LFGY) aims to generate income through derivatives based on some of the most volatile assets available on Wall Street. Since option premiums increase with higher volatility, LFGY has an incredibly high dividend yield of 20.91% and pays it out weekly.

Key ETF Data

Currently, the ETF has an expense ratio of 0.99%, which is normal when compared to other income ETFs like the YieldMax AAPL Option Income Strategy ETF (APLY). When looking at the holdings breakdown, shown in Figure 1, one can see that the ETF mostly holds financials, with more than 50% of its portfolio. Additionally, it is heavily invested in technology stocks or derivatives.

All of this generally speaks to a relatively low level of diversification, which is confirmed when looking at the specific holdings displayed in Figure 2. As can be seen, most exposure is to either cryptos such as iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) or crypto-related stocks such as MicroStrategy Incorporated (MSTR) and Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN). Other stocks like MARA Holdings (MARA) and Robinhood Markets (HOOD) are also heavily reliant on the movements of crypto. Figure 3 also shows this by highlighting the beta and correlation of different stocks to Bitcoin.

Since LFGY was only introduced at the start of this year, it is so far hard to determine the ETF's performance. However, when thinking about the investment strategy, the ETF is quite solid. During upturns, the ETF will, of course, gain less than its underlying stocks / ETFs as it sells options on them, meaning that gains are capped. Nevertheless, it should outperform in times of sideways performance and downturns. Also, it should even increase its already great yield, with rising volatility, as it will receive higher premiums.

Investment Thesis

As we have just seen, the performance of LFGY is strongly dependent on the performance of its underlyings, which again is dependent on the movements of Bitcoin and crypto in general. Because of this, it makes sense to look more closely at the current state of the crypto market. When looking at the crypto fear and greed index, shown in Figure 4, one can instantly see that there currently is a lot of greed in the market. Generally speaking, this should make investors more cautious as it suggests that many potential buyers have already done so at the moment.

Furthermore, Figure 5 shows the implied volatility of 90-day at-the-money options on Bitcoin. As can be seen, it is currently low on a one-year scale. On one side suggests that options are currently cheap, giving LFGY much more potential. On the other hand, it could also suggest a less positive outlook as volatility has normally moved upward from current levels. Figure 6 also shows the daily chart of Bitcoin. While the crypto does currently have strong momentum and is in an uptrend, the resistance at $106k could be a potential initiator for a bounce back. All of this favors a more cautious approach, which LFGY brings to investors.

When looking at the underlying companies, one can also see some risks that favor selling options on them instead of just holding them directly. COIN currently trades at extremely high valuations, with an EV/EBITDA ratio of 78.42 and a P/E ratio of almost 50. The same can be said of MSTR at a forward EV/EBITDA of over 4000 and negative earnings. Furthermore, MSTR has negative revenue growth and an ROE of more than -30%, while COIN has negative EBITDA growth of over -40%. All of this suggests a lot of risk in the crypto and crypto-related company sector, making LFGY an attractive, more conservative bet that is also great for income investors.

Risks

Considering risks, the biggest one when just looking at performance would be a big crash in the crypto sector. While LFGY would outperform the sector, it would still show negative performance. At the same time, a huge rally, while benefiting the ETF, would lead to underperformance. While these two risks have to be considered, LFGY looks interesting in market phases like this that are driven by a lot of uncertainty.

Conclusion

In total, LFGY looks like a very interesting play at the moment, as it gives crypto investors more security in a phase where it is hard to say where the market is going. In a downward phase, LFGY will likely outperform Bitcoin, and the income could be used to buy the dip. In an up phase, the ETF would still show attractive performance, while the most beneficial situation would be for the market to go sideways, in which case LFGY would show strong returns, while the market does nothing. Because of this, LFGY currently gets a buy rating from me.

data.title: Meta Platforms: A Growing Risk Investors Can't Keep Ignoring
data.description:
Following the recent release of Meta Platforms' (NASDAQ:META) Q1 2025 earnings report, investors bid up its stock price as the market gained confidence that the company's AI investments are paying off through better recommendation systems and users' time spent. While this is certainly encouraging, Meta also faces a growing risk amid the rising popularity of generative AI apps, which threaten to expand into the social media space and eat into the company's digital advertising market share over time.

In the previous article, we had discussed how Meta Platforms' conversational AI assistant "Meta AI" is falling behind rivals like ChatGPT in terms of usage volumes, undermining the social media giant's challenge to these new-age AI apps. We also covered the looming risk of Meta potentially having to divest Instagram and WhatsApp due to antitrust litigation, which would be a big blow to the company given how important these apps are to win in the generative AI era.

Since then, a new risk has arisen, with OpenAI contemplating launching its own social media app by leveraging the existing popularity of its ChatGPT assistant and image-generation tool that has gone viral over the past few months.

Meta stock maintains a "hold" rating.

New AI apps Are a Force to Be Reckoned With

When people talk about the rapid growth of OpenAI, they immediately think about the threat it poses to Google Search, as the functionalities of ChatGPT have expanded to Search verticals like "Maps" and "News". Although with OpenAI increasingly pushing out new creative tools like image/ video generators, the rising AI app also threatens to take away time spent on Meta's social media apps like Instagram, Facebook and WhatsApp.

Last month, OpenAI shared that ChatGPT had reached 800 million weekly users. While Meta Platforms has not yet shared how many weekly users its "Meta AI" assistant has garnered, CEO Mark Zuckerberg did mention on the recent earnings call that "there are now almost a billion monthly actives using Meta AI". That's more than Gemini's 350 million monthly users revealed in April, although Meta has still not been able to dethrone ChatGPT as the top AI app despite boasting 3.4 billion daily users across its social media apps.

The reason why the use of ChatGPT has been proliferating at an accelerated pace over the past few months is due to the new image-generation tool it launched at the end of March 2025.

The company debuted the "high-quality" image-generation tool as a way to produce everything from diagrams, infographics and logos to business cards and stock photos. The feature can also use an image as a starting point for art, such as a custom painting of a pet or editing a professional headshot.

…

Images of anime-style renderings of users' uploaded photos have been going viral on X and other social media apps

- CNBC

In just over a week after the feature launched, OpenAI's COO Brad Lightcap shared on April 3rd that:

Over 130 million users have generated more than 700 million images since the upgraded image generator launched in ChatGPT on March 25.

- TechCrunch

This is the kind of viral popularity that Meta wishes it could garner for its own generative AI features that rolled out over the past two years.

In the autumn of 2023, Meta had introduced experimental AI features called "AI Personas", whereby users could interact with digital avatars of celebrities like Snoop Dogg and Kendall Jenner, as well as non-celebrity AI personalities. However, both these experiences have been discontinued following user backlash.

Meta removes AI character accounts after users criticize them as 'creepy and unnecessary'

In July 2024, Meta scrapped its celebrity AI characters and launched AI Studio, a way for people to create their own AI characters that can also be accessed by other users through the messaging functions on Meta's social media platforms.

- NBC News

The social media giant had also launched an "Imagine me" feature for creative selfies in July 2024, following the popularity of the "Lensa AI" app.

However, almost a year after launching these generative AI experiences, executives haven't offered any statistical insights into usage volumes the way OpenAI frequently reveals for new features.

The lack of traction for Meta's own generative AI experiences means it is vulnerable to being competitively challenged by new social media apps that are likely to arise in this new era of generative AI.

In fact, building upon the popularity of ChatGPT and its image-generation tool, there have been reports that OpenAI is considering entering the social media space:

OpenAI, the generative artificial intelligence startup backed by Microsoft (MSFT) and others, is working on building a social network, The Verge reported.

The project is still in the early stages, but an internal prototype includes a social feed that is focused on ChatGPT's image generation, the news outlet added, citing sources familiar with the matter.

- Seeking Alpha News

This presents a massive risk to Meta, which is already struggling to ignite user excitement around its own AI features. If OpenAI's endeavor in the social media space proves successful based off of the already-popular image-generation tool, it could further subvert the time spent on Meta's suite of apps, just like how TikTok has been a big headache over the past several years.

Moreover, OpenAI also made advances last month to facilitate more shopping activities through ChatGPT, by surfacing product recommendations in response to commercial queries from users.

Just like OpenAI, rising AI Search engine "Perplexity" also recently expanded into facilitating commerce activities, and both companies have partnerships with Shopify (SHOP) to buoy these endeavors.

So these new-age AI apps, particularly OpenAI, are incrementally encroaching onto Meta Platforms' territory, as they strive to carve out a slice of the digital advertising market for themselves.

Furthermore, a survey of 12,000 consumers by consultancy firm "Capgemini" revealed that:

More than half (58%) have replaced traditional search engines with Gen AI tools as their go-to for product/service recommendations. 68% of consumers want Gen AI tools to aggregate search results from online search engines, social media platforms, and retailers' websites to provide a one-stop shop for highlighted purchase options.

- Capgemini

The second statistic is particularly concerning, as over two-thirds of surveyed consumers don't want to bother with visiting different social media apps and retail webstores to find the product that they want.

Instead, they are asking for chatbots (and eventually AI agents) to scour through social media pages on apps like Instagram, Facebook, TikTok and Pinterest, as well as other sources, and aggregate product recommendations that fit their queries.

So instead of just humans browsing on social media platforms, over time it could increasingly be AI agents skimming through these apps to serve users' shopping intentions.

Earlier this year, OpenAI already introduced the "Operator" AI agent that works at the browser system level to conduct both research and commercial activities on behalf of users. And Perplexity is anticipated to launch its agentic "Comet" browser this month.

AI Agents facilitating commerce activities for users could indeed distort the digital advertising landscape for companies like Meta Platforms, which have traditionally designed product recommendations and advertisements for humans.

Additionally, while the "Capgemini" citation earlier highlighted that 58% of consumers overall now turn to "Gen AI" tools for shopping queries, this number jumps to 65% for Gen-Z consumers, and 67% for Millennials.

So the threat of OpenAI (and potentially other generative AI start-ups) stepping into Meta Platforms' territory should not be taken lightly.

While social media apps are primarily destinations for social interactions and entertainment, Meta Platforms has been successfully cultivating its apps into prominent shopping destinations as well. However, the rise of new, generative AI-powered chatbots and social networks could throw a spanner into the works of Meta on this front.

Meta Platforms Is Fighting Back

Despite the rise of new generative AI apps, it's not as if commerce activities through social media platforms are going to completely vanish. After all, these platforms have given rise to influencers that are increasingly inducing their followers to engage in shopping activities.

Social media influencers are also becoming popular, with around 7 in 10 Gen Zs learning about new products through them in 2024, up significantly from 45% in 2023.

Platforms such as Instagram and TikTok are also reshaping retail, with over half of consumers discovering new products via social media, up from 32% in November 2022.

- Capgemini

Furthermore, Meta has been leveraging the power of AI to continuously enhance the predictive accuracy of its recommendation systems to display the most relevant content to users on their feeds.

In the last six months, improvements to our recommendation systems have led to a 7% increase in time spent on Facebook, a 6% increase on Instagram, and 35% on Threads. Threads now also has more than 350 million monthly actives and continues to be on track to become our next major social app.

- CEO Mark Zuckerberg, Meta Platforms Q1 2025 earnings call

These remarks ignited investors to push the stock price of META higher post-earnings, as it offered evidence that the social media giant's massive investments into AI are paying off.

Additionally, even if users don't intentionally open Meta's apps for shopping purposes, people spending more time on entertaining content gives the company incremental opportunities to show ads, and also keeps the window open for commerce activities through social media influencers.

Meta undoubtedly has a lot more experience at perfecting recommendation systems than OpenAI and other new-age challengers.

Another area where Meta has a lot more experience is connecting businesses with the right consumers for targeted advertising. The company has been using generative AI to make the advertising process a lot smoother for businesses.

Our goal is to make it so that any business can basically tell us what objective they're trying to achieve like selling something or getting a new customer and how much they're willing to pay for each result, and then we just do the rest.

Businesses used to have to generate their own ad creative and define what audiences they wanted to reach, but AI has already made us better at targeting and finding the audiences that will be interested in their products than many businesses are themselves, and that keeps improving. And now AI is generating better creative options for many businesses as well.

…

In just the last quarter, we are testing a new ads recommendation model for reels, which has already increased conversion rates by 5%, and we're seeing 30% more advertisers are using AI creative tools in the last quarter as well.

- CEO Mark Zuckerberg, Meta Platforms Q1 2025 earnings call

So while OpenAI and Perplexity are taking steps to enhance the commercial potency of their platforms to attract advertising dollars in the future, Meta possesses a treasure of data accumulated over many years that it is now leveraging in the generative AI era, enabling advertisers to target consumers more accurately.

The Investment Case for Meta Platforms

Meta Platforms delivered total revenue growth of 17.5% in Q1 2025. More importantly, Family of Apps revenue grew by over 16%, which actually marks a slowdown from the same quarter last when it grew by almost 26%.

Nonetheless, investors were impressed by Meta's strengthening pricing power for its ad solutions:

The average price per ad increased 10%... Pricing growth benefited from increased advertiser demand, in part driven by improved ad performance. - CFO Susan Li, Meta Platforms Q1 2025 earnings call

Indeed, Meta's massive investments into AI infrastructure is presently paying off in the form of more precise recommendation systems, conducive to more relevant content and ads being shown to users.

Although AI gains are expected to stay limited to recommendation system advancements over the near future, as executives have signaled that they don't expect their generative AI-powered features (such as the "Meta AI" assistant and "AI Studio" which allows users to build their own AI personas) to become monetizable in 2025.

This year the improvements of the business are going to be taking the AI methods and applying them to advertising and recommendations and feeds and things like that. So the actual business opportunity for Meta AI and AI studio and business agents and people interacting with these AIs remains outside of '25 for the most part.

- CEO Mark Zuckerberg, Q4 2024 earnings call

Before Meta can even get to monetize its generative AI features, it will first need to accelerate user engagement, as these experiences across the suite of apps are still not garnering the traction that OpenAI is witnessing.

In terms of EPS growth, Meta is expected to grow at 17.42% CAGR over the next 3–5 years.

While Meta's projected EPS growth rate is greater than that of chief advertising rival Google, it is lower than that of Amazon, which also competes against Meta for advertising dollars.

Based on these projected EPS growth rates, below are the Forward Price-Earnings-Growth (Forward PEG) ratios for the Magnificent 7 stocks.

A Forward PEG multiple of 1x would implicate that a stock is trading at around fair value.

So with the king of AI, Nvidia, trading well below fair value, the other Mag 7 names naturally appear expensive to own.

Although there is a growing narrative that the AI trade will shift from hardware to software stocks this year. But this narrative isn't necessarily backed up by EPS growth projections, as Nvidia's expected 35%+ EPS growth (3–5Y CAGR) still far outpaces the forecasts for the other AI players.

Microsoft, Amazon and Google each possess cloud service platforms which also serve as the "picks and shovels" of this AI revolution. This has enabled MSFT in particular to command a premium valuation relative to the other Mag 7 stocks, with its financial performance buoyed by OpenAI's success as well.

On the other hand, Meta, Google and Amazon are simultaneously at risk from new-age AI apps like OpenAI and Perplexity distorting the way consumers embark on their shopping journeys.

And unlike its chief advertising rivals, Meta Platforms does not have a cloud services business of its own to benefit from during the transformation phase across companies and industries.

Instead, Meta is solely reliant on its generative AI-powered social media features driving user engagement and future ad dollars. So far, this narrative has not yet taken off.

Meanwhile, OpenAI reportedly generated revenue of $3.7 billion in 2024, and expects to bring in a staggering $12.7 billion in 2025. While this still dwarfs in comparison to Meta Platforms, which produced over $164 billion in revenue last year, investors cannot ignore the fast pace at which the new-age AI companies are growing.

Over the past several quarters throughout 2024, the advertising revenue growth rates for Meta, Google and Amazon have been slowing down, following robust growth rates in 2023. Looking forward, any re-acceleration in ad revenue growth rates would depend on their AI features witnessing usage growth among consumers.

High-quality tech stocks with promising growth stories and strong moats tend to trade at Forward PEG multiples of 2x or above.

Although at present, out of the Mag 7 it is Microsoft and Apple that are trading above 2x Forward PEG, while Tesla is in a league of its own at almost 8x.

In order for Meta to be able to command a higher valuation multiple than the 1.44x it is currently trading at, it needs to deliver robust user engagement growth for its latest generative AI experiences, amid the growing competitive threats from new-age AI apps like OpenAI. Any uptrend in usage of features like "AI studio" and "Meta AI", particularly for commercial purposes, would give investors the confidence they need that advertising revenue will continue to grow into the future.

For now, Meta stock maintains a 'Hold' rating.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of -1.02% (Institutional shares) and -1.07% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • The fund’s outperformance of its benchmark was led by select sentiment and fundamental quality insights, which helped drive successful positioning across Europe and Japan. However, U.S. security selection struggled amid changing leadership dynamics.
  • From a sector- and country-positioning perspective, the fund remained largely neutral. It had slight overweight allocations to the information technology and financials sectors, and maintained slight underweight exposures to the consumer discretionary and utilities sectors. The fund had a slight overweight position in the United Kingdom and maintained a slight underweight exposure to the United States.
Contributors Detractors
Sentiment-related insights capturing “smart-money” positioning drove gains. These insights overcame sharp derisking in early March as investors rotated out of previous momentum winners. Fundamental quality measures focused on sustainability of earnings were also top contributors. Other stability-related fundamental insights with a preference for lower volatility stocks and dividend consistency were additive. Collectively, stock selection was strong across Europe through a preference for domestic exposure via the financials sector and the aerospace and defense industry within the industrials sector over those reliant on global trade. These gains were supported by positioning in Japanese banks, driven by accelerating inflation and rising rates expectations. In contrast to positioning abroad, security selection in the United States came under pressure, notably in the IT and industrials sectors. Traditional fundamental valuation insights tracking sales, valuations, and other financial statement data drove losses. Notably, positioning in the IT sector began to evolve toward artificial intelligence adopters over enablers, which helped stem losses in March as large-cap technology winners capitulated. Elsewhere, trend following insights, both macro and analyst and management sentiment, struggled against changing leadership dynamics. Similarly, consumer intent-related sentiment measures evaluating online search trends, social media data, and product-ranking trends all proved wrong-footed.

Further insight

After touching all-time highs in February, global equities declined sharply to end in negative territory. U.S. market stress drove the sell-off as uncertainty about the new administration’s policy agenda simultaneously stoked recession concerns and market volatility. Combined with cooling sentiment toward the AI opportunity, after Chinese companies released rival, low-cost AI models, market leadership rotated as elevated U.S. valuations came under pressure. Unsurprisingly, despite concerns about inflation resulting from tariffs, economic growth worries drove a sharp drop in bond yields. In contrast, international markets proved resilient as investors repositioned for the end of the U.S. exceptionalism theme. European markets were the main beneficiary, despite sluggish economic data and corporate revenues overly exposed to tariff risks.
